title: Why Asteroid Mining Needs Only Three Levers

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Asteroids contain quadrillions of dollars in precious metals. The barrier to mining them is not the resource — it is the cost of the first trip. Three real engineering levers could close that gap.

card.copy: Why Asteroid Mining Needs Only Three Levers

beats.narration_text: The economics: current cost to reach a near-Earth asteroid and return a kilogram of material is roughly $50,000. Platinum group metals — platinum, palladium, rhodium — sell for roughly $30,000 per kilogram. The gap: $20,000 per kilogram — currently unprofitable by 40%.

The quadrillions of dollars in asteroid resources are real — but unreachable at current transport cost. What specific changes would close the $20,000-per-kilogram gap? Are they physically possible, or are they engineering fantasies?

card.copy: Platinum in asteroids: $30k/kg. Cost to get it: $50k/kg. $20k gap. What closes it — and can the physics support it?

beats.narration_text: Lever one: ion propulsion. Chemical rockets carry most of their mass as propellant — the rocket equation demands it. Ion engines use electrical power to accelerate ions to 10 to 100 times the exhaust velocity of chemical rockets. In deep space, once the initial orbit is reached, ion engines are 10 times more fuel-efficient. First-mission cost drops significantly.

production_viz.label: Lever 1: Ion engines — 10–100× exhaust velocity vs chemical. 10× more efficient in deep space.

beats.narration_text: Lever two: orbital mechanics. Asteroids in the right orbits require very little energy to redirect — a small nudge at the right moment, exploiting gravitational geometry, can bring a large asteroid into a stable orbit near Earth. The force required is tiny; the timing precision is the challenge.

production_viz.label: Lever 2: Small nudge at right moment → large redirect. Gravitational geometry.

beats.narration_text: Lever three: compounding infrastructure. The first mission brings back asteroid material. Some of that material — water for propellant, metal for structures — is used to build the second mission cheaper. The second mission enables a third at lower cost still. Each mission produces inputs that reduce the next mission's cost.

production_viz.label: Lever 3: Mission 1 material → Mission 2 cheaper → Mission 3 cheaper still

beats.narration_text: The numbers: Mission 1 at $50,000 per kilogram using Earth propellant. Mission 2 uses asteroid-sourced propellant: roughly $20,000 per kilogram. Mission 10 uses asteroid-sourced propellant plus asteroid-built hardware: $2,000 to $5,000 per kilogram. Below platinum's market price. The curve crosses the revenue line.

production_viz.label: Mission 1: $50k. Mission 2: $20k. Mission 10: $2–5k. Curve crosses revenue.

beats.narration_text: The near-Earth asteroid 16 Psyche is estimated to contain iron and nickel worth 10 quintillion dollars — more than Earth's entire economy. This number is fictional as stated: if that metal reached Earth, the market price of iron would collapse. Asteroid resources are valuable only if extracted slowly enough not to crater the markets.

production_viz.label: 16 Psyche: $10 quintillion nominal. Reality: flood Earth's iron market → price collapses.

beats.narration_text: The realistic target is platinum group metals: scarce on Earth, high-value per kilogram, used in catalysts and electronics. A single platinum-rich asteroid the size of a house block contains more platinum than humanity has ever mined. The market for platinum would absorb it gradually.

production_viz.label: Target: platinum group metals. One house-block asteroid > all platinum ever mined.

beats.narration_text: Three levers: ion engines — 10 times fuel efficiency in space; orbital mechanics — small nudge redirects large asteroid; compounding infrastructure — each mission funds the next. Together they bend the cost-per-kilogram curve below the revenue line. The gap closes.

card.copy: Cost: $50k/kg. Revenue: $30k/kg. Three levers: ion engines → orbital mechanics → compounding infrastructure. Cost curve bends below revenue.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
